source_metadata.title_raw: Theresa L. Stricker - A-20-013 - March 19, 2020 - San Francisco

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 19, 2020

Theresa L. Stricker
Town Attorney
Town of Corte Madera
350 Sansome St. Suite 300
San Francisco, CA 94104

Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-20-213

Dear Ms. Stricker:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Corte Madera Town
Councilmember David Kunhardt regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common
law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. May Councilmember Kunhardt take part in decisions involving ADU ordinance revisions
that revise criteria applying only in the Christmas Tree Hill Overlay District ("CTH"), given that he
owns a home within the portion of the CTH that is subject to accessory dwelling units ("ADUs")
capacity limitations?

2. May Councilmember Kunhardt take part in decisions relating to the Town's response to
the California Department of Housing and Community Development ("HCD") if the HCD finds that
any aspect of Ordinance No. 992 is noncompliant with state law?

3. May Councilmember Kunhardt take part in decisions by the Town Council, including one
or more ordinances that are anticipated to come forward for consideration in 2020 proposing
modifications to the design review standards that apply to residential development applications

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-20-013
Page No. 2

within the CTH given that the existing design review standards within the CTH differ from the
design review standards applicable to residences located elsewhere in the Town?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Because Councilmember Kunhardt's property will be subject to amended development
standards within the proposed ADU ordinance revisions, he has a prohibited conflict of interest and
may not make, participate in making, or use his official positions to influence a governmental
decision regarding those changes.

2. Whether Councilmember Kunhardt may take part in decisions related to the Town's
response to HCD will depend on the nature of the HCD's findings and the response. If
Councilmember Kunhardt needs additional assistance regarding his involvement in decisions
involving a response to HCD, he should seek further assistance identifying the nature of the
resulting from HCD's findings.

3. Because Councilmember Kunhardt's property will be subject to the amended
development standards within the proposed modifications to the design review standards, he has a
prohibited conflict of interest and may not make, participate in making, or use his official positions
to influence a governmental decision regarding those changes.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Councilmember Kunhardt and his spouse own and reside in a single-family home located
within an ADU capacity district number 2 of the CTH. Councilmember Kunhardt's financial interest in
the home exceeds a fair market value of $2,000.

As a Councilmember Kunhardt and his spouse completed an expansion of the home in 2003.
As a result of that expansion, the home now has four off-street parking spaces. Councilmember
Kunhardt is considering converting floor space that was added to the home during the 2003
expansion into a junior accessory dwelling unit ("JADU") or an ADU but has no current plans to do
so. Conversion of that existing space into a JADU or ADU would not be subject to the Town's
ADU capacity limits. As of December 18, 2019, three ADUs exist in ADU capacity district no. 2 in
the CTH, which exceeds the 7% (2 ADU) limit for that capacity district.

ADU Regulations

In 1994, the Town Council made findings about the unique characteristics and public safety
concerns in the Christmas Tree Hill area based on the (1) narrow, steep, and winding roads; (2)
small developed lots with limited off-street parking; (3) severe fire hazard and high fuel loading
resulting from heavy vegetation and older wooden structures; (4) limited emergency access and
emergency evacuation options; and (5) limited ability of the existing infrastructure facilities to
accommodate additional development.

Based on those findings, in 1994 the Town Council adopted an ordinance establishing an
overlay zoning district, known as the CTH, and established capacity limitations on the number of
ADUs that may be developed within a significant portion of the CTH. The Town Council

File No. A-20-013
Page No. 3

reaffirmed those findings in 2016 when the Council last amended other aspects of the Town's ADU
regulations.

The Town's current regulations divide a significant portion of the CTH into eleven ADU
capacity districts, each with its own allotted limit for certain types of ADUs. The capacity limits
apply only to ADUs that are created by building a new accessory structure or expanding a primary
residence or an existing accessory structure. These capacity limitations do not restrict conversion of
existing attached or detached floor area into an ADU or JADU.

Approximately 30 of the 280-285 residential parcels within the CTH lie outside of the ADU
capacity districts and thus are not subject to the ADU capacity limits that apply elsewhere within
the CTH. As of December 18, 2019, there are approximately 277 to 282 residential units in the
portion of the CTH that is subject to ADU capacity limitations. Approximately 250-255 of those
residential units are single-family residences and the remaining 27 residential units are ADUs. In
Corte Madera as a whole, there are approximately 4,131 residential units, of which approximately
3,206 are single-family residences.

Recent State Legislation Addressing ADUs

Since the Town Council last amended the ADU regulations in December 2016, the State
Legislature enacted several bills that limit municipalities' discretion to restrict the addition of ADUs
and JADUs that meet certain specific criteria. The most recent of these bills took effect on January
1, 2020. Under the new legislation, municipalities adopting ordinances that impose new or amended
ADU/JADU regulations must submit their ordinances to the HCD for compliance review within
sixty days of adoption. (Gov. Code, § 65852.2(h)(1).) As part of its review, HCD may make written
findings as to whether the ordinance complies with state law. (Ibid.)

Under this process, if HCD finds that a municipality's ADU/JADU ordinance does not
comply with state law, HCD must provide the municipality with up to 30 days to respond to HCD.
(Id. at § 65852.2(h)(2)(A).) After the local entity responds, it may either amend its ordinance to
comply with HCD's findings or adopt the ordinance again without changes to its operative
provisions but with findings to establish how the ordinance complies with state law. (Id. at §
65852.2(h)(2)-(3).)

Proposed ADU Revisions including Ordinance No. 992

At its regular meeting on December 3, 2019, the Town Council introduced Ordinance No.
992 to repeal and replace the Town's existing ADU/JADU regulations. The Ordinance is intended
to bring the Town's existing ADU/JADU regulations into compliance with new state law and make
other changes to the Town's existing ADU/JADU regulations. Of particular significance here, the
Ordinance as introduced includes a number of regulations that apply only in the CTH.
Councilmember Kunhardt recused himself from that discussion.

As introduced, the Ordinance readopts the Town's existing ADU capacity limitations in the
CTH for detached and attached ADUs that create additional gross floor area and reaffirms the
findings previously made to support those limitations. As introduced, the Ordinance does not
change the existing ADU capacity limits set forth under the Town's Municipal Code. Additionally,

File No. A-20-013
Page No. 4

the Ordinance eliminates a loophole in the Town's existing ADU capacity limits within the CTH, which allows a property owner in the CTH to evade the existing ADU capacity restrictions by doing in two steps what the owner cannot do in one step. Specifically, under current regulations, a property owner may first add additional square footage to his or her existing home or add a new detached accessory structure (like a pool house or studio) through the Town's regular permit application process and then, after the addition or new accessory structure has been constructed, immediately submit an application to convert the new addition or structure into an ADU without regard to the ADU capacity limit.

The Ordinance proposes to close this loophole in the existing ADU capacity limitations by adding a new ADU regulation that restricts conversion of any new square footage added to a primary residence or detached accessory structure into an ADU or JADU. This restriction would apply within the capacity limitation districts in the CTH for five years after the affected addition or structure was constructed.

As stated in a subsequent email, the Town Council adopted the Ordinance at its January 21, 2020 meeting. Once again, Councilmember Kunhardt recused himself from this item and did not participate in the discussion or decision. Pursuant to new state law that took effect in January 2020, Town staff submitted the Ordinance to HCD for review. Should HCD find that any aspect of the Ordinance does not comply with current state law, the Ordinance will come back for further consideration by the Town Council. It is also possible that HCD will only file that elements of the Ordinance that apply Town-wide do not comply with state law.

Additionally, at the January 21, 2020 meeting, the three remaining Town Councilmembers directed staff to bring back for consideration at a future Town Council meeting a new ordinance to broaden the types of ADUs that are subject to the existing ADU capacity limitations in the CTH. In particular, the Town Council directed staff to draft an ordinance that would make ADUs converted from existing floor space (for example, basements, garages and existing accessory structures), and all JADUs, subject to the existing ADU capacity limitations in the CTH. Under Ordinance No. 992, ADUs created from existing floor space and all JADUs are exempt from the ADU capacity limitations. Even with those exemptions from the capacity limitations, eight of the eleven ADU capacity districts in the CTH are at maximum capacity, including capacity District 2 where Councilmember Kunhardt's home is located.

Design Review Standards

Like many other jurisdictions, the Town requires design review in certain circumstances to ensure that proposed residential or commercial construction projects that exceed certain established thresholds are consistent with Town's development standards, policies and objectives for thoughtful planning and design. The Town's existing municipal code, as implemented by Town staff, establishes thresholds for when certain residential construction projects are subject to design review. These thresholds vary within the Town depending on the applicable zoning. When a design review permit is required, discretionary findings must be affirmatively made before the project application may be approved. None of the relevant design review provisions are affected by the changes in state law regarding ADUs.

File No. A-20-013
Page No. 5

Compared to other residential zoning districts, the Town has very different design review
thresholds for residential projects within the CTH. With limited exceptions, design review is
required for all residential construction projects in the CTH. As set forth in the Town's Municipal
Code, approval of a residential project subject to design review currently requires findings that (1)
the project conforms with the general plan or community plan; (2) the project will not unnecessarily
remove trees and natural vegetation, and will preserve natural landforms; (3) the project will not
significantly affect the views of nearby residences; and (4) the proposed site plan is harmonious
with existing and future adjacent development.

Residential development projects within the CTH must also satisfy additional design review
findings including findings that (1) the project protects the natural and existing land forms and
vegetation of the hillside and ridges located on the property; (2) the grading plan is designed to
retain the natural features of the land and cuts and fills are minimized; (3) the location of the
building site is selected to reduce the visibility of the development from the rest of the community;
(4) all slopes are planted or otherwise protected from erosion; and (5) the project does not adversely
affect the health, safety, or welfare of persons in the area.

Design Review Standard Revisions

Town staff plans to bring forward ordinances in 2020 to amend the Town's residential
design review standards, possibly modifying design review permit thresholds and/or the required
discretionary findings and making other appropriate changes to encourage early engagement and
communication with impacted neighbors, such as enhanced noticing procedures.

Staff anticipates that, as a part of this process, the Town Council will consider whether to
modify the existing the CTH residential design review thresholds or required findings to make them
more consistent with the standards applicable to residential projects located outside of the CTH.
Staff anticipates that proposed changes to the CTH residential design review standards would be
brought forward to the Town Council in a CTH-specific ordinance that would not address proposed
changes to residential design review standards that apply elsewhere within the Town.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their duties
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100 prohibits a public
official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. See section 87103
provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act,
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more
of the public official's interests that is distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public
generally.

Section 87103 also describes the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise under
the Act. As pertinent to the facts presented and the decision at issue, an official has a potentially
disqualifying conflict if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material effect on any

File No. A-20-013
Page No. 6

interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b).) Accordingly, we must analyze the potential effect of the decisions at issue on
Councilmember Kunhardt's interest in his residential property.

Regulation 18701(a) provides:

(a) Financial Interest Explicitly Involved: A financial effect on a financial interest is
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

In all other cases, Regulation 18701(b) provides that if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

As applicable for purposes of Councilmember Kunhardt's residence, an effect on the property will be material under Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) if the decision "[i]nvolves the adoption or amendment of development criteria applicable to the parcel."

Under the facts presented, the proposed ADU ordinance revisions will establish specific criteria that applies only in the CTH. The design review standards revisions will also entail specific criteria that applies only to residential development applications in the CTH. Thus, the proposed decisions involve the amendment of development criteria applicable to Councilmember Kunhardt's property, and the effect of the decisions on the parcel is reasonably foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and material under 18702.2(a)(1). Accordingly, Councilmember Kunhardt has a disqualifying conflict of interest and may not take part in decisions relating to the adoption of proposed ADU regulations or the design review standards that apply to residential development applications within the CTH.

Decisions After HCD Review

You indicate that some provisions of Ordinance No. 992 apply Town-wide. While you have not specifically identified the provisions that may be considered, you have indicated that in the event HCD finds that provisions of Ordinance No. 992 that apply Town-wide do not comply with state law, the Council may take up amendments to the ordinance. Generally, the question of whether Councilmember Kunhardt may take part in decisions related to the Town's response to HCD, will depend on the specific nature of the HCD's findings and the response.

However, we do note that to the extent that the Town will consider an amendment that can be "segmented" from the decisions that effect the CTH, Councilmember Kunhardt may be able to take part in the subsequent decisions under the public generally exception.

File No. A-20-013
Page No. 7

Segmentation

The segmentation rule in Regulation 18706 recognizes that the conflict of interest rules of
the Act are applied on a decision-by-decision basis. For example, even related decisions can be
analyzed separately under limited circumstances such that where an official has a conflict of interest
with one of the related decisions, he or she may participate in other decisions so long as they are not
inextricably interrelated. For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably interrelated"
when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of
another decision.

Regulation 18706 provides the method for segmentation of a governmental decision:

"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following
conditions apply:

"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken
down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;

"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented
from the other decisions;

"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered
first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's
participation in any way; and

"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been
made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening
of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was
disqualified.

Public Generally

Commonly referred to as the "public generally exception," Regulation 18703(a) sets forth
the general rule:

A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest
is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his
or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant
segment.

For purposes of the exception, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25
percent of (1) the businesses or nonprofit entities; (2) the real property, commercial or residential;
or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).) With respect to the

File No. A-20-013
Page No. 8

decision at issue, the "significant segment of the public" is 25 percent of the residences within the
City Council district the Council Member represents. (Regulation 18703(d).)

To the extent that the Town will consider an amendment to the Ordinance No. 992 that
applies Town-wide and the decision can be properly segmented under Regulation 18706(a), the
public generally exception may allow Councilmember to take part in the decisions. However, we
caution that each decision must be reviewed on a case by case basis and we can only provide
general and informal assistance based upon the facts provided. If there are any questions regarding
segmentation or the public generally exception as applied to any specific decision before the Town
Council, Councilmember Kunhardt may wish to seek formal advice identifying the decision at
issue.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

Zachary W. Norton

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:aja

design review standards applicable to residences located elsewhere in the Town?

within ADU capacity district number 2 of the CTH. Councilmember Kunhardt’s financial interest in 
the home exceeds a fair market value of $2,000.

As a result of that expansion, the home now has four off-street parking spaces. Councilmember

Under this process, if HCD finds that a municipality’s ADU/JADU ordinance does not

(Id. at § 65852.2(h)(2)(A).) After the local entity responds, it may either amend its ordinance to

At its regular meeting on December 3, 2019, the Town Council introduced Ordinance No.

sections.analysis:
The Act’s conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their duties 
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial 
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100 prohibits a public 
official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 
provides that a public official has a “financial interest” in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, 
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more 
of the public official’s interests that is distinguishable from the decision’s effect on the public 
generally.  
 
Section 87103 also describes the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise under 
the Act. As pertinent to the facts presented and the decision at issue, an official has a potentially 
disqualifying conflict if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material effect on any   
 
 
interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) Accordingly, we must analyze the potential effect of the decisions at issue on 
Councilmember Kunhardt’s interest in his residential property.  
Regulation 18701(a) provides: 
(a) Financial Interest Explicitly Involved: A financial effect on a financial interest 
is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party 
in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official’s 
agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves 
the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or 
other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any 
governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).  
In all other cases, Regulation 18701(b) provides that if a financial effect can be recognized 
as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A 
financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable, however, if the financial 
result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official’s 
control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. 
As applicable for purposes of Councilmember Kunhardt’s residence, an effect on the 
property will be material under Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) if the decision “[i]nvolves the adoption or 
amendment of development criteria applicable to the parcel.”  
 
Under the facts presented, the proposed ADU ordinance revisions will establish specific 
criteria that applies only in the CTH. The design review standards revisions will also entail specific 
criteria that applies only to residential development applications in the CTH. Thus, the proposed 
decisions involve the amendment of development criteria applicable to Councilmember Kunhardt’s 
property, and the effect of the decisions on the parcel is reasonably foreseeable under Regulation 
18701(a) and material under 18702.2(a)(1). Accordingly, Councilmember Kunhardt has 
disqualifying conflict of interest and may not take part in decisions relating to the adoption of 
proposed ADU regulations or the design review standards that apply to residential development 
applications within the CTH.  
Decisions After HCD Review  
 
 
You indicate that some provisions of Ordinance No. 992 apply Town-wide. While you have 
not specifically identified the provisions that may be considered, you have indicated that in the 
event HCD finds that provisions of Ordinance No. 992 that apply Town-wide do not comply with 
state law, the Council may take up amendments to the ordinance. Generally, the question of 
whether Councilmember Kunhardt may take part in decisions related to the Town’s response to 
HCD, will depend on the specific nature of the HCD’s findings and the response.  
 
However, we do note that to the extent that the Town will consider an amendment that can 
be “segmented” from the decisions that effect the CTH, Councilmember Kunhardt may be able to 
take part in the subsequent decisions under the public generally exception.    
 
 
Segmentation 
 
The segmentation rule in Regulation 18706 recognizes that the conflict of interest rules of 
the Act are applied on a decision-by-decision basis. For example, even related decisions can be 
analyzed separately under limited circumstances such that where an official has a conflict of interest 
with one of the related decisions, he or she may participate in other decisions so long as they are not 
inextricably interrelated. For purposes of this regulation, decisions are “inextricably interrelated” 
when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of 
another decision. 
 
Regulation 18706 provides the method for segmentation of a governmental decision: 
 
 “(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a 
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following 
conditions apply: 
 
 “(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken 
down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in 
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
 
 “(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented 
from the other decisions; 
 
 “(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered 
first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official’s 
participation in any way; and 
 
 “(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been 
made, the disqualified public official’s participation does not result in a reopening 
of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was 
disqualified. 
 
 
Public Generally 
  
Commonly referred to as the “public generally exception,” Regulation 18703(a) sets forth 
the general rule: 
 
A governmental decision’s financial effect on a public official’s financial interest 
is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official 
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his 
or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant 
segment.  
 
For purposes of the exception, a “significant segment of the public” consists of at least 25 
percent of (1) the businesses or nonprofit entities; (2) the real property, commercial or residential; 
or (3) the individuals within the official’s jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).) With respect to the  
 
 
decision at issue, the “significant segment of the public” is 25 percent of the residences within the 
City Council district the Council Member represents. (Regulation 18703(d).)  
 
To the extent that the Town will consider an amendment to the Ordinance No. 992 that 
applies Town-wide and the decision can be properly segmented under Regulation 18706(a), the 
public generally exception may allow Councilmember to take part in the decisions. However, we 
caution that each decision must be reviewed on a case by case basis and we can only provide 
general and informal assistance based upon the facts provided. If there are any questions regarding 
segmentation or the public generally exception as applied to any specific decision before the Town 
Council, Councilmember Kunhardt may wish to seek formal advice identifying the decision at 
issue.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May Councilmember Kunhardt take part in decisions involving ADU ordinance revisions 
that revise criteria applying only in the Christmas Tree Hill Overlay District (“CTH”), given that he 
owns a home within the portion of the CTH that is subject to accessory dwelling units (“ADUs”) 
capacity limitations? 
2. May Councilmember Kunhardt take part in decisions relating to the Town’s response to 
the California Department of Housing and Community Development (“HCD”) if the HCD finds that 
any aspect of Ordinance No. 992 is noncompliant with state law? 
3. May Councilmember Kunhardt take part in decisions by the Town Council, including one 
or more ordinances that are anticipated to come forward for consideration in 2020 proposing 
modifications to the design review standards that apply to residential development applicationswithin the CTH given that the existing design review standards within the CTH differ from the 
design review standards applicable to residences located elsewhere in the Town?

CONCLUSION: 1. Because Councilmember Kunhardt’s property will be subject to amended development 
standards within the proposed ADU ordinance revisions, he has a prohibited conflict of interest and 
may not make, participate in making, or use his official positions to influence a governmental 
decision regarding those changes.  
 
2. Whether Councilmember Kunhardt may take part in decisions related to the Town’s 
response to HCD will depend on the nature of the HCD’s findings and the response. If 
Councilmember Kunhardt needs additional assistance regarding his involvement in decisions 
involving a response to HCD, he should seek further assistance identifying the nature of the 
resulting from HCD’s findings.  
 
 
3. Because Councilmember Kunhardt’s property will be subject to the amended 
development standards within the proposed modifications to the design review standards, he has a 
prohibited conflict of interest and may not make, participate in making, or use his official positions 
to influence a governmental decision regarding those changes.
